title: Blanksquare
description: Blanksquare is a composable privacy stack for EVM wallets and DeFi apps. The Shielder SDK lets any ERC-20 token be deposited into the Shielder, a mixer that uses zk-SNARKs to break linkability between deposit and withdrawal addresses. Designed for integration into wallets and DeFi protocols with 600-800ms proving on consumer hardware.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Deposit and withdraw amounts are public on-chain at the pool boundary — a third-party observer sees that some unknown account withdrew a specific amount (e.g. 20 USDT). There is no confidential in-pool transfer that hides values, so transaction amounts are not private.

citations.cited_text: A 3rd party observer only sees that some unknown account just withdrew `20` USDT from the shielder.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Deposits and withdrawals are individually visible on-chain, but a deposit transaction cannot be linked to its withdraw transaction, so the depositing and withdrawing addresses cannot be tied together. Privacy comes from breaking deposit-to-withdraw linkability, not from hiding the sender and receiver of a transfer.

citations.cited_text: There is no way to link the deposit transaction to the withdraw transaction.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: At the entry/exit boundary the deposit/withdraw transaction names the specific ERC-20 token being moved, making the asset type visible to chain observers.

citations.cited_text: Alice calls the `deposit` method of the `Shielder` contract, sending `100 USDT` along the call.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Entering the pool requires calling the Shielder contract directly — a protocol-specific entry point identifiable on-chain as Blanksquare usage. The deposit transaction is distinguishable from an ordinary ERC-20 transfer.

citations.cited_text: Alice calls the `deposit` method of the `Shielder` contract, sending `100 USDT` along the call.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Application-layer Solidity contracts deployed on host chains. Mainnet on Base, with testnets on Base Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Hyperliquid testnet (HyperEVM). Finality inherits from each host chain.

citations.cited_text: Base 0x064A67a5484DF6baf36be42F9554d45E7741dCFf

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: The user holds a single 256-bit master seed. ZK-ID, per-transaction nullifiers, and trapdoors are deterministically derived from it. The proof system is Ultraplonk with KZG commitments, instantiated via Halo2 over BN256.

citations.cited_text: we introduce a deterministic secret management scheme that allows the user to keep just one secret seed and derive all the remaining secrets out of it.
The user starts by generating a master seed: `seed` with say 256 bits,

### Proof System 

We use the Ultraplonk proof system instantiated using a KZG commitment scheme.
For the time being we decided to use BN256, at least until support for the BLS12 family of curves is added to Halo2.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Deposit is a single Shielder.deposit() transaction. No protocol-imposed queue, challenge window, or time-lock is documented. Host-chain block latency is not a deposit waiting period.

citations.cited_text: Sends a deposit transaction to the Shielder smart contract

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Withdraw is a single Shielder.withdraw() transaction submitted via a relayer. No queue, exit window, or time-lock is imposed by the protocol. Host-chain block latency is not a withdraw waiting period.

citations.cited_text: The relayer pays gas and broadcasts the transaction to the network.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Blanksquare runs on permissionless host chains, so any valid deposit or withdraw can be included. Relayers are an optional convenience — users can self-broadcast the signed Shielder transaction directly, at some cost to privacy — so no permissioned entity gates operations. The multisig that can upgrade or brick the verifier is an availability and upgrade risk captured under Upgradeability.

citations.cited_text: if this was done just by the user itself, then it would lead to privacy loss

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Relayers exist as a convenience broadcasting layer but are optional — users can self-broadcast the signed shielder transaction directly. No external committee, oracle, or staked validator set is required between the user and the host chain.

citations.cited_text: parties that post shielder transactions to chain, on behalf of normal users

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: The Shielder contract on Base mainnet exposes a withdraw entry point callable at any time using only host-chain consensus and the on-chain zk-SNARK verifier. The deployment sits behind an ERC1967 proxy, so a verifier upgrade could affect future exits, but the baseline exit path runs without a protocol-imposed timelock or admin gate.

citations.cited_text: Base 0x064A67a5484DF6baf36be42F9554d45E7741dCFf

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: The Base mainnet Shielder is a UUPS ERC1967 proxy, so its implementation, including the verifier circuit and the rest of the shielder behaviour, can be replaced. Upgrade authorization is gated by _authorizeUpgrade onlyOwner, and the owner resolves on-chain to a 2-of-N Ownable2Step multisig that can also pause the contract. The power to swap the implementation rests with that multisig.

citations.cited_text: function _authorizeUpgrade(address) internal override onlyOwner {}

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Blanksquare generates ZK proofs in-browser via a WASM cryptography engine. The deposit and withdraw flows construct the proof on the user device before submitting the transaction or handing it to a relayer.

citations.cited_text: Generates ZK proof client-side using WASM cryptography engine

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Proving runs in-browser via the WASM engine, so no external prover sees the witness inputs, and relayers receive only the signed proof and public inputs, not the private witness. The exception is the governance-gated Anonymity Revoker, which can deanonymize a flagged deposit after the fact. This is a compliance feature rather than a prover leak.

citations.cited_text: Generates ZK proof client-side using WASM cryptography engine
A governance process decides whether to deanonymize the user behind this deposit.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Blanksquare's Shielder contract on Base mainnet was deployed in July 2025, placing it under one year of mainnet operation as of mid-2026. Additional testnet deployments exist on Base Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Hyperliquid testnet.

citations.cited_text: Base 0x064A67a5484DF6baf36be42F9554d45E7741dCFf https://base-shielder-relayer-v3.blanksquare.dev

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: The cryptographic stack relies on primitives that fall to Shor's algorithm. The proof system is Ultraplonk instantiated with a KZG commitment scheme, and the elliptic curve choice is BN256, pending support for the BLS12 family in Halo2 — both pairing-based constructions broken by a quantum adversary. Asymmetric encryption uses ElGamal over the Grumpkin elliptic curve, whose discrete-log hardness is likewise broken by Shor.

citations.cited_text:
### Proof System 

We use the Ultraplonk proof system instantiated using a KZG commitment scheme.
For the time being we decided to use BN256, at least until support for the BLS12 family of curves is added to Halo2.
For asymmetric encryption we use the familiar ElGamal cryptosystem, however to make it snark-friendly we need a specific choice of the group we work with. Specifically let $$G$$ be the Grumpkin

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Compliance via Anonymity Revokers operates at the application layer of the Shielder protocol. Revocation is triggered by a governance vote, not by host-chain consensus.

citations.cited_text: A governance process decides whether to deanonymize the user behind this deposit.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: A governance process votes on whether to invoke the Anonymity Revoker against a specific deposit. The Anonymity Revoker entity executes the decryption only after governance approval.

citations.cited_text: A governance process decides whether to deanonymize the user behind this deposit.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Two parallel mechanisms exist. Anonymity Revokers perform governance-triggered involuntary post-hoc deanonymization (Other). The Shielder SDK also exposes voluntary user-controlled viewing keys that grant chosen counterparties read access to activity (Selective disclosure).

citations.cited_text: A governance process decides whether to deanonymize the user behind this deposit.
the user has access to special "viewing keys" which allows them to view all their past activity in shielder

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: The Anonymity Revoker's encrypted commitment is sealed into the account-creation circuit at first deposit, anchoring future deanonymization to that initial proof. Subsequent withdrawals do not run additional compliance gates.

citations.cited_text: Find the `key(id)`of the user (account) who created this transaction.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Users can self-view their own past activity using their viewing keys. They can voluntarily share a viewing key with any chosen third party. Governance can additionally invoke the Anonymity Revoker to deanonymize a specific deposit, an involuntary protocol-level disclosure path.

citations.cited_text: the user has access to special "viewing keys" which allows them to view all their past activity in shielder
A governance process decides whether to deanonymize the user behind this deposit.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Viewing keys can be granted by the user to any chosen party at any time. The Anonymity Revoker's commitment is sealed at first deposit, but the user-controlled viewing-key channel lets the user grant, withhold, or share viewing material after the fact.

citations.cited_text: The user can also share this key with any other entity that they want to grant view access to their trace.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Shielder verifies zk-SNARK proofs in the on-chain verifier contract rather than relying on majority vote or social attestation. Inclusion and ordering are inherited from the host chain.

citations.cited_text: uses the technology of zk-SNARKs to enable private payments and private interactions with Defi.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The deployed Base Shielder, the Shielder SDK, and the relayer are published in the zkOS-monorepo under the Apache-2.0 licence, which is where the live contract source lives. The newer blanksquare-v2-monorepo, holding the Noir circuits and generated verifiers, carries no licence.

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: The Shielder maintains an append-only Merkle tree of note commitments plus a growing nullifier set. No pruning mechanism is documented and on-chain state grows with cumulative pool usage.

citations.cited_text: set of elements of type `Scalar` whose purpose is to invalidate old notes

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: The client syncs the latest Shielder state and constructs proofs locally. No server-side index keyed to the user is documented. The WASM cryptography engine drives both proving and chain scanning.

citations.cited_text: Sync to ensure up-to-date state

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Each note is a UTXO-style commitment stored as a leaf in the Merkle tree, with a hashed nullifier published on spend to prevent double-spend. Notes carry an account identifier but the on-chain leaf is treated as a UTXO commitment.

citations.cited_text: Each leaf of the `notes` Merkle Tree is a hash of a `note`

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: The notes Merkle tree, the nullifier set, and the encrypted note payloads are stored in the Shielder smart contract on the host chain. The state is recoverable by any full node.

citations.cited_text: Each leaf of the `notes` Merkle Tree is a hash of a `note`

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Access to external, but limited choice of DeFi protocols
properties.notes: Shielded assets are traded on public DeFi venues rather than a dedicated shielded DEX, so reaching protocols such as Uniswap or Cowswap is supported through the public route. A built-in Shielder-SDK integration with various DEXes is planned but not yet shipped, so today the path runs through the public withdraw route rather than a native in-pool DeFi runtime, which bounds what shielded balances can reach directly.

citations.cited_text: any public protocol can be used to trade shielded assets, such as Uniswap or Cowswap
An out-of-the-box integration with various DEXes will be provided as part of Shielder-SDK in the future.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Blanksquare's current surface is shielded payments — deposit and withdraw. The Shielder SDK enables developers to integrate this primitive into wallets and apps but does not extend the protocol's own programmability beyond payments.

citations.cited_text: a clear programmatic interface to Shielder, enabling the development of privacy-enhanced apps and wallets
